assumptions: The dividend receivable is an intragroup receivable/payable between consolidated group entities.
The question concerns hedge accounting in consolidated financial statements under IFRS 9.
No facts indicate that the receivable is part of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A risk component may be designated if it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
conditions: The receivable is an intragroup monetary item with FX exposure not fully eliminated on consolidation
The designated FX component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented at inception
The hedge effectiveness and hedge ratio requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: The receivable's FX component can be included in a documented hedge relationship only if the consolidation-level exception and hedge accounting criteria are satisfied.

source_contributions.summary: Foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item may qualify in consolidated financial statements when the FX effects are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Hedged items must be reliably measurable; risk components must be separately identifiable and reliably measurable; documentation and effectiveness criteria are required.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: As a general rule, only items with a party external to the reporting entity can be designated as hedged items in consolidated financial statements. Intragroup items are excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: Absent the specific foreign-currency exception, the intragroup dividend receivable cannot be designated as the hedged item in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Only external-party assets, liabilities, firm commitments or highly probable forecast transactions are generally eligible hedged items in consolidated financial statements.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 allows certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged for foreign currency risk if that risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. That model does not fit an already declared dividend with a recognised receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: The recognised dividend receivable should not be analyzed as a forecast intragroup transaction hedge.

source_contributions.summary: A highly probable forecast intragroup transaction may qualify as a hedged item for FX risk in consolidated financial statements if the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 addresses hedges of foreign currency risk arising from net investments in foreign operations, ie net assets, not a declared intragroup dividend receivable. On the stated facts, this is a different hedge accounting model.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not use the net investment hedge model for the dividend receivable on the provided facts.

source_contributions.summary: Net investment hedge accounting applies to foreign currency risk on net investments in foreign operations and related qualifying hedging instruments.
